content:
Git

Github

Programming

Software Development

Open Source

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve just started programming, you’ve probably heard people say, “Push it to GitHub.” But what exactly are Git and GitHub, and why do developers use them every day?
This guide will help you understand the fundamentals of Git and GitHub with practical examples, even if you’ve never used them before.

What is Git?
Git is a distributed version control system that helps developers track changes in their code over time.
Think of Git as a “save history” for your projects. Instead of saving multiple folders like:

• Project_Final

• Project_Final2

• Project_Final_Updated

• Project_Final_ReallyFinal
Git stores every change in an organized timeline, making it easy to revisit previous versions whenever needed.
Why Use Git?
Git helps you:

• Track every change in your project

• Collaborate with other developers

• Restore older versions if something breaks

• Work on new features without affecting the main project

• Keep your project organized

What is GitHub?
GitHub is a cloud platform that hosts Git repositories online.
While Git manages your project locally on your computer, GitHub allows you to:

• Back up your repositories online

• Share code with others

• Collaborate on open-source projects

• Showcase your work to recruiters and employers
In simple terms:

• Git = Version Control

• GitHub = Online Repository Hosting

Installing Git
Download Git from the official website:
https://git-scm.com/
After installation, verify it by running:
git --versionExample output:
git version 2.50.1
Configuring Git
Set your username and email:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"Verify the configuration:
git config --list
Creating Your First Repository
Create a new folder:
mkdir MyProject
cd MyProjectInitialize Git:
git initGit creates a hidden .git folder that stores the repository history.

Understanding the Git Workflow
A typical Git workflow follows these steps:
Working Directory
↓
git add
↓
Staging Area
↓
git commit
↓
Repository
↓
git push
↓
GitHub
Your First Commit
Create a file:
README.mdAdd content:
Hello Git!Check the repository status:
git statusStage the file:
git add README.mdOr stage everything:
git add .Commit the changes:
git commit -m "Initial commit"
Connecting to GitHub
Create a new repository on GitHub.
Then connect it:
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/MyProject.gitVerify:
git remote -v
Uploading Your Code
Push your project:
git branch -M main
git push -u origin mainYour project is now available on GitHub.

Everyday Git Commands
Check status:
git statusView commit history:
git logSee recent commits in one line:
git log --onelineCheck differences:
git diffRemove staged files:
git restore --staged filenameDelete a file:
git rm filenameRename a file:
git mv oldname newname
Understanding Branches
Branches let you develop features independently without affecting the main project.
Create a branch:
git branch feature-loginSwitch to it:
git checkout feature-loginOr:
git switch feature-loginCreate and switch together:
git checkout -b feature-loginList branches:
git branch
Merging Branches
Merging is the process of combining changes from one branch into another.
Basic Merge
Switch to the branch you want to merge into (usually main):
git checkout mainMerge another branch:
git merge feature-loginGit will combine the changes from feature-login into main.

Fast-Forward Merge
If no new commits were made on main, Git simply moves the pointer forward:
main: A --- B
feature: C --- DAfter merge:main: A --- B --- C --- D
Merge Commit
If both branches have new commits, Git creates a merge commit:
main: A --- B --- E
feature: C --- DAfter merge:A --- B --- E
\ /
C --- D
Handling Merge Conflicts
Sometimes Git cannot automatically merge changes.
Example conflict:
<<<<<<< HEAD
Hello from main branch
=======
Hello from feature branch
>>>>>>> feature-loginTo resolve:

• Edit the file and choose the correct content

• Remove conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>)

• Stage the file:
git add filename
• Complete the merge:
git commit
Working with Remote Repositories
Fetch changes without merging:
git fetch originPull changes (fetch + merge):
git pull origin mainPush changes:
git push origin main
Cloning a Repository
Copy an existing repository:
git clone https://github.com/username/project.git
Undoing Changes
Sometimes mistakes happen. Git makes it easy to fix them.
Undo changes in a file:
git restore filenameUndo last commit (keep changes):
git reset --soft HEAD~1Undo last commit (discard changes):
git reset --hard HEAD~1Revert a commit safely:
git revert <commit-id>
Stashing Changes
If you’re not ready to commit but need to switch branches:
git stashApply stashed changes:
git stash applyList stashes:
git stash list
What is a Pull Request?
A Pull Request (PR) is a request to merge your changes into another branch.
Typical workflow:

• Create a branch

• Make changes

• Commit changes

• Push the branch

• Open a Pull Request

• Get the changes reviewed

• Merge into the main branch
Pull Requests are essential for team collaboration and open-source contributions.

Common Git Errors
“Nothing to commit”
Your files haven’t changed or haven’t been staged.
“Repository not found”
Check the repository URL and your access permissions.
“Merge conflict”
Git couldn’t automatically combine changes from different branches. You’ll need to resolve the conflicting sections manually before committing.
Authentication failed
If you’re using GitHub, use a Personal Access Token (PAT) instead of your account password when prompted.

Best Practices

• Commit frequently with meaningful messages

• Keep commits focused on one logical change

• Pull before pushing when working with others

• Use feature branches for new work

• Write a helpful README

• Add a .gitignore file to avoid committing temporary files or secrets

Quick Command Cheat Sheet
git init
git status
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git log --oneline
git branch
git checkout -b feature
git merge feature
git remote add origin <url>
git push -u origin main
git pull origin main
git clone <repository-url>
git stash
git reset --soft HEAD~1
git revert <commit-id>
Final Thoughts
Learning Git and GitHub is one of the most valuable skills for any developer. They help you write code with confidence, collaborate effectively, and maintain a reliable history of your work.
The best way to learn is by practicing. Create small projects, experiment with branches, make commits often, and don’t be afraid to make mistakes — Git is designed to help you recover from them.
Happy coding!
